case_name: Removal of 1,2,3-Trichloropropane from well water for municipal drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing 1,2,3-Trichloropropane from well water intended for drinking water use.

input_data.scenario_background: A community water supplier operates a groundwater well field and has detected 1,2,3-Trichloropropane in one production well above drinking water screening and regulatory concern levels. The utility wants a proven treatment approach that can be integrated into a conventional small municipal treatment setting with reliable day-to-day operation. Raw water is otherwise relatively clean and does not require extensive solids treatment, so the main design question is selection of the best-established process for the organic contaminant.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable reduction of 1,2,3-Trichloropropane to meet applicable drinking water requirements and provide stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: use proven and publicly documented treatment technology
preserve finished water quality without extensive post-treatment complexity

core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb 1,2,3-Trichloropropane from groundwater using a well-established physical removal process
core_unit_functions.lead_lag_vessels: provide operational redundancy, breakthrough control, and reliable effluent compliance
core_unit_functions.post_disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in the drinking water distribution system

applicability_rationale: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane is a trace hydrophobic synthetic organic contaminant for which activated carbon is a well-established drinking water treatment option
raw well water has low turbidity and low organic carbon, which supports efficient carbon adsorption performance
a lead-lag GAC configuration is widely used in municipal VOC and SOC control to improve reliability and simplify breakthrough management
this option matches the utility preference for proven technology and moderate operational complexity
potential_risks: carbon exhaustion and contaminant breakthrough require routine monitoring and timely media replacement
unexpected increases in competing organic matter could shorten bed life
spent carbon handling and reactivation logistics must be managed properly

core_unit_functions.packed_tower_aeration_or_air_stripping: transfer volatile organic contaminant mass from water to air
core_unit_functions.off_gas_control_if_required: manage stripped contaminant in the vapor phase where required by air regulations or community acceptance

applicability_rationale: air stripping is an established technology for many volatile organic compounds in groundwater treatment
it can be considered for 1,2,3-Trichloropropane where site-specific mass transfer design confirms adequate performance
well water quality is otherwise clean, which simplifies upstream conditioning needs
potential_risks: 1,2,3-Trichloropropane is less easily stripped than more volatile chlorinated solvents, so tower size and design demands may be substantial
off-gas treatment may be required, adding complexity and cost
cold weather and flow variability can affect stripping efficiency

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_uv_hydrogen_peroxide: generate hydroxyl radicals to degrade recalcitrant organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.biologically_stable_post_treatment: remove residual oxidant byproducts and stabilize finished water quality

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can be technically capable of degrading 1,2,3-Trichloropropane when adsorption or stripping are not preferred or need polishing support
the low DOC in the well water is favorable for radical efficiency relative to high-organic waters
this is a recognized treatment family for difficult trace organics, but it is less conventional than adsorption for a simple single-contaminant well-water case
potential_risks: higher operator skill and process monitoring are needed compared with GAC
energy use and chemical consumption are greater than for simple adsorption
formation of transformation byproducts requires evaluation and verification

core_unit_functions.synthetic_resin_adsorption: adsorb target organic contaminant using specialty polymeric media
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain fines or media carryover and polish the treated water

applicability_rationale: specialty adsorptive resins can remove hydrophobic organic contaminants and may be considered as an alternative adsorptive approach
the clean well water is suitable for fixed-bed adsorption service
however, GAC has broader public-sector operating history and stronger recognition as the conventional choice
potential_risks: media selection is site-specific and may have less full-scale municipal precedent than GAC for this contaminant
replacement frequency and disposal requirements may be uncertain early in operation

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis_or_nanofiltration: provide broad contaminant rejection through pressure-driven membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: high-pressure membranes can reduce many dissolved contaminants and could technically address 1,2,3-Trichloropropane
they may be reasonable only when multiple dissolved contaminants also require simultaneous treatment
for a single trace organic in otherwise good-quality well water, this is usually unnecessarily complex relative to proven adsorption
potential_risks: high energy demand and membrane replacement costs
concentrate management burden for a contaminant present at trace concentration
more extensive post-treatment and operational oversight than needed for this case

preferred_rank_pattern: air_stripping may be considered but should usually rank below GAC for TCP due to lower stripping favorability
high_pressure_membranes should not rank first under a proven-technology preference and otherwise clean well water

must_reflect: preference for proven municipal drinking water technology
moderate energy sensitivity favoring adsorption over energy-intensive oxidation or membrane systems
simple robust operation with clear breakthrough monitoring
avoidance of unnecessarily complex residual or concentrate management

must_contain: why GAC is appropriate for trace organic removal in clean groundwater
why lead_lag adsorption operation improves reliability for drinking water compliance
why air stripping is less favored than GAC for 1_2_3_trichloropropane
why membrane options rank lower for a single-contaminant well-water scenario

should_mention: adsorbent breakthrough and media replacement scheduling
possible off_gas management needs for air stripping
byproduct or operational complexity concerns for advanced oxidation
concentrate disposal concerns for membrane treatment
